goal: focus on fundamental technique with a compact 2-week plan (6 sessions) and measurable improvements in technique, timing, and flavor
constraints: 3 sessions per week, 45–90 minutes per session
hands-on practice with a compact 2-week plan (6 sessions)
equipment limited to stove, oven, a good chef's knife, a cutting board, one pan, and one pot; no specialized gadgets
3 sessions per week, 45–60 minutes per session
Portions and repetition tailored for a single-person household (single-serving portions, minimal waste)
Budget-focused: favor affordable, easy-to-find ingredients over specialty items
In-session practice drills integrated into prep to minimize extra ingredients
Each practice session aims for total ~30–40 minutes
Progress tracking through daily drills and weekly checks integrated into the plan (2–5 minute daily drills and 5–10 minute weekly checks)
Workspace constraints: small kitchen counter with clutter from mail and a plant; quick declutter steps required before cooking and photographing
Starter spice pantry: create a minimal 8–12 item pantry focused on Italian cooking, sized for single-person budget
Prioritize long-shelf-life choices (whole spices preferred) when practical in the starter pantry
decisions: Budget-focused approach for 2-week plan; prioritize affordable, easy-to-find ingredients
Adopt three daily drills and two weekly checks for progress tracking; include a printable log option
Incorporate pan-sear technique practice (including salmon) focusing on crust formation and moisture management as part of the 2-week plan
Provide a first shopping list with quantities (grams/oz) tuned to cooking frequency
Offer an 6–8 spice starter list focused on Italian cooking for a small-budget, single-person household
open: Knife drill — onion or carrot: Dice one onion (or one carrot) the same way each time. Metric: time (seconds) + uniformity rating 1–5. Example: Knife 00:45 / 4
Hot-pan sear test — one small piece: Heat pan and sear one small piece (tofu slice, shrimp/scallop, or egg). Metric: release? (Y/N) + crust rating 1–5. Example: Sear Y / 4
Salt/acid taste test — quick palate training: Cook one spoonful of a vegetable; adjust salt and acidity; Metric: which improved most? (Salt / Acid / Neither). Example: Taste → Acid
Emulsion stability — Make a small vinaigrette or mayo. Wait 10 minutes. Metric: stable? (Y/N) + balance rating 1–5. Example: Mayo Y / 3
Starch test — Cook single serving of rice or pasta; in the end judge texture. Metric: texture 1–5 or separate/not gummy. Example: Rice 4
Release test — note whether the salmon releases cleanly from the pan (Yes/No). Metric: release (Y/N) + crust rating 1–5. Example: Release Y / 4
Crust rating — rate the exterior crust for crispness on a 1–5 scale. Example: Crust 4
Acid brightness practice — add a tiny splash of vinegar or lemon near the end of cooking to brighten flavors; compare with control; metric: brightness rating 1–5

Two-minute salting test drill — Salt one slice (e.g., zucchini or eggplant) and leave the other unsalted. Wait 10–15 minutes, pat dry, then sear both identically. Metrics: crust/browning 1–5; texture 1–5. Record result. Repeat with salt timing variations (salt just before cooking) to compare results.
